coredata.dc:title: Pristine and holey graphene quantum dots: Optical properties using time independent and dependent density functional theory
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Abdelati, Mohamed A.
Fadlallah, Mohamed M.
Gamal, Yosr E.E-D.
Maarouf, Ahmed A.
coredata.dc:description: The zero bandgap of graphene limits its utilization in optoelectronics. Quantum confinement in finite-size graphene structures, such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and holey GQDs, may offer a path for generating a wide range of HOMO-LUMO gaps. Edge terminations and pore passivations in these structures provide further means to alter their optical properties. In this work, we study the structural stabilities and optical properties of GQDs and holey GQDs with different sizes, edge terminations, and pore passivations, using density functional theory (DFT) as well as time dependent DFT. We find that the optical spectra of GQDs depend primarily on their size. Edge termination has a small influence on the spectra; shifting the absorption peaks by ~ 0.2 eV, which gets smaller for larger GQDs. Creation of pores in GQDs lead to the emergence of new peaks in their absorption spectra. Pore passivation seems to have the biggest effect on the absorption spectra of holey GQDs, while their termination leads to slight shifts in their peaks. Our results can be used to develop promising materials for many applications, such as biological sensors, and optoelectronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Quantum dot
Density functional theory
Time dependent density functional theory
Optical properties
Engineering bandgap
Broadening optical spectrum
The optical filter

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge the use of the computing resources through the computational Cy-Tera Project ( NEA YΠOΔOMH/ΣTPATH/0308/31 ), which is co-funded by the European Regional Development Fund and the Republic of Cyprus through the Research Promotion Foundation . We would like to acknowledge the support of the supercomputing facility at the Bibliotheca Alexandrina, Alexandria, Egypt. A. Maarouf would like to acknowledge the use of the resources of the Supercomputing Laboratory at KAUST, Thuwal, KSA.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimal structures of GQDs with H termination; (a) C24H12, (b) C54H18, (c) C96H24, and (d) C 150 H30. The corresponding structures with F termination are also considered. Optimal configuration of holey GQDs; (e) H6–C144H30, (f) H12–C126H30. Other holey GQDs where H is replaced by F for pore passivation and edge termination are also considered. (g) O3–C146H30, (h) O6–C138H30, and (i) O6–C126H30. The corresponding structures where O is replaced by N passivation are also considered. Yellow, blue, and red spheres represent C, H, and O atoms, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Absorption spectra for: (a) benzene (C6H6), and (b) hexafluorobenzene (C6F6) as compared to the experimental results [49,50]. The corresponding structures and DOSs are the inset figures.
Absorption spectra for GQDs: (a) C24H12, (b) C24F12, (c) C54H18, (d) C54F18,(e) C96H24, (f) C96F24, (g) C 150 H30, and (h) C 150 F30. The corresponding structures and their DOS are the inset figures.

Absorption spectra for holey GQDs; H6–C144H30 (a), H12–C126H30 (b), H6–C144F30 (c), H12–C126F30 (d), F6–C144H30 (e), F12–C126H30 (f), F6–C144F30 (g), and F12–C126F30 (h). The corresponding structures and DOSs are the inset figures.
Absorption spectra for holey GQDs; O3–C144H30 (a), N3–C144H30 (b), O6–C126H30 (c), N6–C126H30 (d), O6–C126H30 (e), and N6–C126H30 (f). The corresponding structures and DOSs are the inset figures.

head.ce:title: Pristine and holey graphene quantum dots: Optical properties using time independent and dependent density functional theory

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Institute of Laser Enhanced Sciences, Cairo University, ElGiza, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Institute for Research and Medical Consultations, Imam Abdulrahman Bin Faisal University, Dammam, 31441, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Research and Medical Consultations

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Physics Department, Institute for Research and Medical Consultations, Imam Abdulrahman Bin Faisal University, Dammam, 31441, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The zero bandgap of graphene limits its utilization in optoelectronics. Quantum confinement in finite-size graphene structures, such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and holey GQDs, may offer a path for generating a wide range of HOMO-LUMO gaps. Edge terminations and pore passivations in these structures provide further means to alter their optical properties. In this work, we study the structural stabilities and optical properties of GQDs and holey GQDs with different sizes, edge terminations, and pore passivations, using density functional theory (DFT) as well as time dependent DFT. We find that the optical spectra of GQDs depend primarily on their size. Edge termination has a small influence on the spectra; shifting the absorption peaks by ~ 0.2 eV, which gets smaller for larger GQDs. Creation of pores in GQDs lead to the emergence of new peaks in their absorption spectra. Pore passivation seems to have the biggest effect on the absorption spectra of holey GQDs, while their termination leads to slight shifts in their peaks. Our results can be used to develop promising materials for many applications, such as biological sensors, and optoelectronic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the last two decades, graphene has been the subject of extensive experimental and theoretical research work [1–3] due to its high mobility, superior thermal conductivity [4], exceptional mechanical properties [5], and high optical transparency [6]. However, pristine graphene has a zero bandgap, which restricts its electro-optical applications. Semiconducting graphene structures have been created through surface functionalization [7], applied mechanical fields [8], and nano-patterning (graphene nanomesh) [9]. In general, modifying of the graphene structure may change its electronic properties, leading to the opening of a bandgap.

Finite-size graphene structures, known as graphene flakes, or graphene quantum dots (GQDs), have a HOMO-LUMO gap that arises from quantum confinement. GQDs have been synthesized by many experimental methods [10–12], and can have many topologies [13–17]. Because of their chemical inertness, low toxicity, photoluminescence, conversion luminescence, and biocompatibility, GQDs are good candidates for many applications, such as bio-imaging [13,14], disease diagnosis [13], drug delivery [13], photodetection, broadband modulators [18], electrochemical sensing [15], catalysis [16], photovoltaics [17], fuel cells [15], and metal ion detection [19].

In recent years, there has been a lot of studies on the optical activity of GQDs. Experimental and theoretical studies [20–22] show that GQDs have a broadband absorption spectrum, making them attractive fluorescence materials in the ultraviolet, visible, and near-infrared regions [20–22]. The optical properties of GQDs depend on their intrinsic properties, such as size, number of layers, shape, and edge termination [23–26]. Substitutional doping can change the energy gap of GQDs and consequently their optical properties [27–30].

The electronic and optical properties of hexagonal GQDs with hydrogen termination were calculated using density functional theory (DFT) [31]. The plasmon resonance in rectangular and hexagonal GQDs with hydrogen termination was calculated using time dependent DFT (TDDFT) [32]. Recently, the tight binding method has been employed to calculate the optical conductivity of GQDs of different shapes and sizes [33]. The effect of vacancies and porphyrin-like vacancies on the spectral properties of GQDs have also been studied [34,35]. The electronic properties of holey (porous) GQDs with nitrogen- and boron-passivation have also been investigated [36]. The holey GQDs structures can be considered as multivacancy GQDs [34], or graphene nanomesh [37,38] quantum dots.

In this work, we present a systematic study of the optical properties of hexagonal GQDs and holey GQDs with different sizes, terminations, and pore passivations. We consider 3 different sizes, four passivations (H, F, O, and N), and two terminations (H and F). The paper is organized as follows: in section II we present the computational models and equilibrium structures. Section III has the optical properties of the studied GQDs. The optical properties of holey GQDs is presented in Section IV, followed by our summary and conclusion in section V.

The optical properties calculations are performed using the plane wave basis set as implemented in the Quantum ESPRESSO package [39]. The generalized gradient approximation with Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhoff [40] is used as the exchange-correlation functional. The energy cutoff of the wavefunctions is 45 Ry. We structurally relax our systems until the force on each atom becomes less than 0.001 Ry/Bohr. To avoid the interaction between GQDs in the adjacent supercells, a vacuum distance of 12 Å in x, y and z directions is used. The optical spectra of the systems are calculated using two different approximations: 1- an independent particle approximation approach to describe the single-particle excitations, and 2- a time dependent DFT (TDDFT) method, calculating the absorption spectra in linear-response without computing empty states and the equations are solved in time or frequency domain [41]. We find no effect for the polarization here of the optical response of the hexagonal GQDs and holey GQDs, which agrees with previous results [32].

Dipoles with different directions at the GQD edge are expected to yield different spectra, especially for small size GQDs. The edge dipole direction varies according to the electronegativity of the edge terminating species as compared to that of carbon. Reasonable choices of monovalent species that give different dipole directions are thus hydrogen and fluorine. Fig. 1 (a–d) shows four H-terminated GQDs: C24H12, C54H18, C96H24, and C 150 H30. The fluorine terminated systems have similar structures, with H replaced by F. Holey GQDs have H and F edge terminations, with four pore passivations (F, H, O, and N). Fig. 1(e) shows an H-terminated holey GQD, with a pore created by removing 6 carbon atoms, followed by pore passivation with 6 H atoms (H6–C144H30). Fig. 1(f) shows the second H-terminated holey GQD (H12–C126H30), where 24 C atoms are removed. Passivation with F gives similar structures, with different pore edge bond lengths (as explained below). For O-passivated holey GQDs (Fig. 1(g–i)), we have 3 pores, obtained by removing 6, 12, and 24 C atoms (O3–C146H30, O6–C138H30, and O6–C126H30, respectively). The N-passivated systems have similar structures, with O replaced by N. Pore sizes of all structures are reported in Table 1 .

The chemical stability is calculated through the electrophilicity index (ω) [42,43]:
(1)
ω =
μ
2
2 η
,
where η and μ are the hardness [44,45] and the chemical potential, respectively, and they are functions of the energies of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (E
LUMO ) and the highest occupied molecular orbital (E
HOMO );
(2)
μ = −
E
H O M O
+
E
L U M O
2
,
(3)
η =
E
L U M O
−
E
H O M O
2
.

The stability of GQDs decreases as the number of C atoms increases. The most stable structures will have the lowest electrophilicity index (ω). We notice that H-terminated GQDs have higher stabilities than the corresponding F-terminated GQDs. For holey GQDs, as the number of removed C atoms increases the stability decreases for the studied passivations (except for O6–C126H30). We find that N3–C146H30 is a metallic system due to its odd number of electrons, which agrees with previously reported results for N passivated graphene nanosheets [46]. As for the chemical hardness parameter, it increases as the number of C atoms decreases in a GQD, which agrees with previous work [47,48]. Because of their low η, C 150 H30(F30), H12–C126H30, O6–C138H30, and N6–C126H30 are the most reactive structures. We also notice that the energy gap of GQDs decreases as the size of GQDs increases, which is in good agreement with published results [47].

In Fig. 2 , we show the optical properties of two basic systems, benzene (C6H6), and hexafluorobenzene (C6F6). The distinct peaks of the absorption coefficient of C6H6 appear at 5.12 eV (242 nm) for DFT, and 6.8 eV (182 nm), 9.7 eV (128 nm), and 11.2 eV (111 nm) for TDDFT. The DFT peak is related to the transition from HOMO to LUMO levels of benzene, as shown (inset) in the density of states (DOS). The TDDFT peak at 6.8 eV (182 nm) is in good agreement with the experimental results [49,50], as well as previous theoretical work [51]. The time dependent calculations show a shift in the absorption peaks to higher energy as compared to the corresponding DFT calculations. Hexafluorobenzene, C6F6, has its absorption peaks at 4.78 eV (259 nm) for DFT, and 6.5 eV (191 nm), 9.6 eV (129 nm), and 11.2 eV (111 nm) for TDDFT. Similar to benzene, the TDDFT absorption peaks are in good agreement with experimental [52,53] and theoretical [52] results. We notice that the number of absorption peaks of hexafluorobenzene is larger than those of benzene, which can be attributed to a large number of F states within the same energy range.

For bigger GQDs, with a hexagonal shape, the number of carbon atoms grows quadratically, while the number of terminating atoms (H or F) increases linearly. Fig. 3 shows the absorption spectra of various studied GQDs. For C24H12 (Fig. 3(a)), the main DFT peaks are at 2.79 eV (444 nm) and 5.4 eV (230 nm), while the TDDFT peaks are at 3.7 eV (335 nm) and 6.2 eV (200 nm). For the corresponding GQD with F termination (Fig. 3(b)), the peaks are located at 2.49 eV (498 nm) and 5.3 eV (234 nm) for DFT, and at 3.4 eV (365 nm) and 5.7 eV (218 nm) for TDDFT. Compared to the absorption spectrum of C6H6 and C6F6, the absorption spectra of C24H12 and C24F12 are shifted towards lower energy. The first peak in the DFT calculation is related to the HOMO-LUMO gap, which is less than the corresponding HOMO-LUMO gap of benzene and hexafluorobenzene. The decrease in the HOMO-LUMO gap of C24F12 as compared to the corresponding gap of C24H12 leads to a redshift in the first absorption peak of C24F12. Other smaller peaks can be attributed to the large delocalized π-orbitals for C24H12 and C24F12 [32]. The TDDFT absorption peaks are blueshifted as compared to those of the DFT calculation. We notice that GQDs have two distinct peaks due to the quantum confinement occurring in a GQD, whereas graphene has only one distinct peak at 6.8 eV [32,54].

For the C54H18 GQD (Fig. 3(c)), the DFT peaks are redshifted as compared to the previous GQD. The first peak is located at 1.9 eV (653 nm) which corresponds to the HOMO-LUMO gap. For TDDFT, there are three distinct peaks which are located at 2.5 eV (496 nm), 4.0 eV (310 nm), and 5.4 eV (230 nm). As for the F terminated GQD (Fig. 3(d)), the DFT peak is located at 1.7 eV (729 nm), i.e. lower than the first peak of the corresponding H-terminated GQD. The TDDFT peaks appear at 2.4 eV (517 nm), 3.8 eV (326 nm), and 5.3 eV (234 nm). The spectral broadening of the C54H18 (F18 is wider than that of the C24H12 (F12).

For the C96H24 GQD (Fig. 3(e)), the DFT peaks are shifted towards the lower energy, and the spectrum is wider than that of C24H12 (Fig. 3(a)) due to the presence of more states. The HOMO-LUMO gap leads to a first peak at 1.3 eV (954 nm). The corresponding F-terminated GQD (Fig. 3(f)) has a similar absorption spectrum. Compared to C24F12 (Fig. 3(b)), the peaks above 10 eV disappear. The TDDFT calculation expands the spectrum to span the 1.6 eV–5.5 eV range, and increases the number of distinct peaks compared to the DFT spectrum of C24F12. The distinct peaks are more broadened than those of the previous structures. For H termination the peaks are located at 1.6 eV (775 nm), 3.2 (387 nm) eV, and 5.2 eV (238 nm). For F termination the peaks are at 1.5 eV (827 nm), 3.2 eV (387 nm), and 5.1 eV (243 nm).

Next we discuss our largest studied GQD, C 150 H30 (F30) (Fig. 3(g and h)). The whole DFT absorption spectrum is redshifted for both terminations. The main absorption peaks of both structures appear at about 1 eV (1240 nm). For TDDFT, the absorption spectra are further broadened by about 0.3 eV as compared to the spectra of the smaller structures. The distinct peaks for both terminations are at 1.3 eV (954 nm), 3.0 eV (413 nm), and 5.2 eV (238 nm). The redshift of the spectrum increases as the size of graphene nanostructure increases which is essentially because the π plasmon resonance mode is a long-range charge transfer excitation [32]. As we see, changing the terminating species mostly affects the absorption spectra of smaller GQDs, as the number of terminating atoms is comparable to that of the carbon atoms.

We now discuss the calculated optical properties of holey GQDs. Many configurations of holey GQDs are considered (Fig. 1(e–i)). We create our holey GQDs systems starting from the largest GQD in this study, C 150 H30. We consider three pore sizes, two terminations (H and F), and four pore passivations (H, F, O, and N), giving us a total of 14 holey GQDs. After structural optimization, we find the average bond lengths of H(F)–C is 1.08 Å (1.35 Å) for the H(F)-passivated and terminated holey GQDs, while for O(N)-passivated holey GQDs the average O(N)–C bond length is 1.44 Å. These bond lengths are in good agreement with previous work [37,38].

Fig. 4 (a) shows the absorption spectrum of H6–C144H30. As compared to C 150 H30, Fig. 3(d), the spectrum is blueshifted, with the main peak of the DFT calculation at 1.14 eV, consistent with the fundamental gap indicated by the DOS (inset). Smaller peaks exist at higher energies up to 6 eV. For TDDFT, the spectrum is further blueshifted compared to the DFT calculations, with an increased broadening compared to the pristine GQD structure. The H6–C144H30 has only two distinct peaks at 2.0 eV (620 nm) and 4.5 eV (275 nm), while the pristine GQD has three distinct peaks. The first peak is blueshifted with respect to the corresponding peak of the pristine GQDs. However, the second peak is redshifted compared to the third peak of pristine GQD.

The H12–C126H30 holey GQD (Fig. 4(b)) has a bigger pore, with a size of 8.25 Å. All its absorption peaks are redshifted compared to its parent GQD and to the H6–C144H30. The main DFT peak is at 0.68 eV, matching the fundamental gap shown in the DOS (inset), while the TDDFT peaks are at 1.3 eV (954 nm), 3.8 eV (326 nm), and 4.8 eV (258 nm), with a shoulder at 6.8 eV (182 nm). Furthermore, the spectrum is more broadened by about 1 eV. This shows that the pore size of GQDs can be used to change the absorption spectra of GQDs.

To study the effect of the terminating species on the optical properties of holey GQDs, we construct two holey GQD structures with fluorine termination and hydrogen passivation: H6–C144F30 and H12–C126F30 (Fig. 4(c and d)). The average C–F bonds are 1.35 Å for the small and large pore GQDs, respectively. The F terminating atoms are coplanar with the carbon atoms. Compared to the corresponding H-terminated GQDs, we notice that they have similar DFT absorption spectra, with a slight redshift of about 0.1 eV. The main DFT peaks match the energy gaps of their systems, as shown by the DOS (insets). Similar changes occur in the TDDFT spectra, where we see a qualitative resemblance with the H-terminated GQD spectra, with a redshift of ~ 0.2 eV for lowest lying peaks, and ~ 0.5 eV for peaks located above ~ 2 eV (Table 1). We also notice that no significant peak broadening occurs.

The pore passivating species may change the optical properties of GQDs. To investigate this, we construct two GQDs with F pore passivation and H termination: F6–C144H30 and F12–C126H30 (Fig. 4(e and f)). The F atoms are out of the GQD plane in an alternating way, due to repulsive interaction arising from their strong electronegativity. Compared to their H-passivated relatives (Fig. 4(a and b)), we see a significant change in the absorption spectra, suggesting that they have a bigger dependence on pore passivation than on edge termination. The DFT peaks agree with the energy gaps of the systems (DOS shown in insets). The positions of the TDDFT peaks (Table 1) of the F6–C144H30 GQD are altered more than those of the F12–C126H30 GQD, compared to the H-passivated corresponding structures. This can be due to the smaller pore of the F6–C144H30, where the pore F atoms interact more strongly than in the bigger pore GQD, causing a more significant change in the energy and hence absorption spectra. The weak effect of the GQD termination on the spectra is confirmed by considering the fourth permutations of the base holey GQD; F6–C144F30 and F12–C126F30 (Fig. 4(g and h)). Similar to what we observed before, changing the termination causes a slight redshift in the absorption spectra, especially for the GQD with the bigger pore. Overall, our results suggest that the pore passivation plays a more significant role in determining the optical properties of holey GQD than does the edge termination, especially for GQDs with smaller pores where the passivating species interact more strongly.

The small pore GQDs considered so far are constructed by removing 6 carbon atoms and subsequently passivating the dangling bonds by monovalent elements (H and F). One can also consider passivation by divalent elements, in which case 3 atoms are needed for passivation. Fig. 5 (a) shows the absorption spectrum of the O3–C144H30 GQD. The optimized structure has an average C–O distance of 1.44 Å. The main DFT peak is located at 1.08 eV (1148 nm), which is supported by the DOS (inset). The TDDFT peaks are at 2.2 eV (563 nm), and 4.7 eV (264 nm), with a small shoulder at 6.0 eV (207 nm). Replacing O with N gives a metallic system, N3–C144H30 (Fig. 5(b)), due to the odd number of electrons in the structure, which agrees with previous studies [46].

A bigger pore size can be constructed by removing 12 carbon atoms, with subsequent passivation by 6 divalent atoms. This allows us to study two more holey GQDs, O6–C138H30 and N6–C138H30 (Fig. 5(c and d)). The DFT spectrum of the O6–C138H30 GQD is redshifted compared to the smaller pore O3–C144H30 GQD. The system has a HOMO-LUMO gap of 0.64 eV, causing the main DFT peak. The TDDFT spectrum is also shifted towards lower energy, with absorption peaks at 1.2 eV (1033 nm), 3.6 eV (344 nm), and 5.4 eV (230 nm). The corresponding system with N passivation has its absorption peaks relatively blueshifted, with the DFT peak at 1.17 eV, and the TDDFT peaks at 2.2 eV (564 nm) and 5.0 eV (248 nm).

A larger pore GQD can be formed by removing 24 C atoms, giving a pore that can be passivated by 6 divalent atoms. Passivation with O and N gives two holey GQDs. The first, O6–C126H30 (Fig. 5(e)), has a DFT peak at 0.8 eV, while its TDDFT peaks are located at 1.4 eV (886 nm), 3.4 eV (365 nm), and 4.5 eV (276 nm). The N passivated system (Fig. 5(f)) has a small gap of 0.08 eV, giving a DFT peak of equal value, and TDDFT peaks at 2.1 eV (590 nm), 3.8 eV (326 nm), and 4.7 eV (264 nm).

The optical properties of pristine and holey graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with different sizes, terminations (H, and F), pore sizes (2.86 Å to 8.28 Å), and pore passivations (H, F, O, and N) are studied using density functional theory (DFT) and time dependent DFT (TDDFT). For all studied structures, we find that the TDDFT absorption spectrum is shifted towards higher energy (blueshift) compared to the corresponding DFT absorption spectrum. The spectra are also redshifted for the F terminated structures as compared to the H terminated structures of small sized pristine GQDs. For larger GQDs, termination seems to have no effect on their electronic and optical properties. The HOMO-LUMO gap decreases as the size of the GQDs increases. For holey GQDs, the absorption spectrum depends primarily on the pore size and pore edge passivation, with a much weaker dependence on the GQD termination. Pore size and pore edge passivation may thus allow for some control over the optical properties of GQDs. This can be utilized in many biological applications such as cell imaging and DNA detection, as well as in electro-optical devices, such as solar cells.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the use of the computing resources through the computational Cy-Tera Project (NEA YΠOΔOMH/ΣTPATH/0308/31), which is co-funded by the European Regional Development Fund and the Republic of Cyprus through the Research Promotion Foundation. We would like to acknowledge the support of the supercomputing facility at the Bibliotheca Alexandrina, Alexandria, Egypt. A. Maarouf would like to acknowledge the use of the resources of the Supercomputing Laboratory at KAUST, Thuwal, KSA.
